skill.name: ai-image-generation
skill.description: Generate AI images through the inference.sh CLI using FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, ImagineArt, and related image models.
skill.summary: Create and edit AI images with inference.sh image generation models.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified a high-risk pipe-to-shell installer at SKILL.md line 22. Most Ruby backtick findings are false positives from Markdown code fences and inline command formatting, but the skill still depends on external CLI execution and network services.

high_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer Instruction
high_findings.description: The quick start downloads a remote installer from https://cli.inference.sh and pipes it directly to sh before login. This pattern executes network-delivered code without review or integrity verification, which can compromise the user environment if the endpoint, transport, or installer is abused.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present on line 22 and has clear execution impact. No malicious intent is confirmed, but the unsafe installer pattern is real.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Drives Core Functionality
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash access for infsh commands and provides many infsh app run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it sends prompts and image URLs to external inference services and depends on remote model execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and example commands show intended external command use. The risk is contextual, not evidence of malware.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Skill Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The related skills section recommends npx skills add commands that fetch additional skill packages. These commands expand the trusted code surface beyond this single audited file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx installation examples are explicit and could install additional content. They appear related to the same vendor, so the concern is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by Markdown code fences and inline command formatting. I found command examples, but no Ruby code or dynamic backtick execution in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visible Markdown examples and tables, not executable Ruby source. Some examples still represent commands a user may run, which is handled in separate findings.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation and User Inputs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings reference inference.sh documentation, an installer endpoint, and placeholder image URLs. The installer URL is handled as high risk; the remaining URLs are ordinary documentation or example inputs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The linked URLs are directly visible and mostly informational. They do not show credential exfiltration or hidden network destinations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptographic algorithm finding points to SKILL.md line 3, which begins the YAML description block. No evidence found for weak cryptographic algorithm usage in that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is not code and contains no cryptographic API or algorithm name. This is a strong false positive based on the inspected file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer command pipes a remote HTTPS response directly to sh. Publish should require a safer installation method with checksum verification or package-manager instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell pipeline is present in the quick start. This is a known dangerous pattern even when the vendor is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Backed Image Processing Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples submit prompts and image URLs through infsh app run commands. Users should treat prompts and source image URLs as data sent to an external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly invoke hosted model apps and include prompt or image_url fields. This is expected behavior, but it creates privacy and data handling risk.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast access to several image generation models without learning each provider interface. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concise inference.sh CLI prompts for image creation and editing.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to generate images from text prompts with inference.sh model app IDs.
Lists supported image models, including FLUX, Gemini, Grok, Seedream, Reve, ImagineArt, and Topaz.
Provides example commands for image-to-image workflows such as upscaling and image stitching.
Guides users toward model choices for text rendering, cinematic images, LoRA use, and upscaling.
Uses the infsh CLI through the declared Bash permission for image model execution.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, an account login, and working network access.
The quick start installer uses a risky pipe-to-shell command that should be replaced before publication.
Generated image quality, latency, and availability depend on external model providers.
Prompts and image URLs may be sent to external services through inference.sh.

use_cases.title: Create Marketing Visual Drafts
use_cases.description: Generate product scenes, campaign concepts, and social graphics from structured prompts.

use_cases.title: Explore Concept Art Directions
use_cases.description: Compare models for landscapes, characters, posters, and cinematic references during early visual planning.

use_cases.title: Prepare Image Processing Tasks
use_cases.description: Run documented commands for upscaling, stitching, and model selection during asset production.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-image-generation skill to create a simple prompt for a product photo of a ceramic mug on a clean studio background.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Model selection for a visual goal
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-image-generation skill to recommend an inference.sh image model for a poster with accurate text and explain the choice briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Create Campaign Variations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-image-generation skill to produce three distinct prompts for a summer launch image, each with a different style and aspect ratio.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Image Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-image-generation skill to plan a workflow that generates a hero image, upscales it, and stitches two supporting images into one banner.

output_examples.input: Generate a product image for a black water bottle.
output_examples.output: A concise prompt for a studio product photo, plus a recommended FLUX model command to run with inference.sh.

output_examples.input: I need a poster with readable words.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to use a text-capable image model such as Reve or Seedream, with prompt guidance for typography.

output_examples.input: Make this image larger and combine two results.
output_examples.output: A two-step workflow that uses the Topaz upscaler example first, then the image stitching app.

best_practices: Review installer commands before running them and prefer verified package sources.
Avoid sending private images, unreleased product details, or sensitive prompts to external services.
Choose models based on the visual requirement, such as text rendering, LoRA style control, or upscaling.
anti_patterns: Do not run remote shell installers in production environments without verification.
Do not include secrets, customer data, or confidential URLs in image generation prompts.
Do not assume every listed model supports the same aspect ratios, quality levels, or editing features.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps users run inference.sh image generation and editing models through documented CLI examples.

faq.question: Does it generate images locally?
faq.answer: No. The examples use inference.sh model apps, so execution depends on external network services.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The quick start pipes a remote installer directly to sh, which executes downloaded code without verification.

faq.question: Can it upscale images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes an example for the Topaz image upscaler app.

faq.question: What data should users avoid sending?
faq.answer: Users should avoid private images, secret URLs, customer data, and unreleased product details.
